---
title: "How Outcome Definition Changes Ecological Inference in Prescription-Based Epidemiology: A Natural Experiment"
author:
  - name: "Haruki Saito"
    affiliation: "Department of Epidemiology, Fukushima Medical University School of Medicine, 1 Hikarigaoka, Fukushima-shi, Fukushima 960-1295, Japan"
    corresponding: true
    email: "m211039@fmu.ac.jp"
  - name: "Tetsuya Ohira"
    affiliation: "Department of Epidemiology, Fukushima Medical University School of Medicine, 1 Hikarigaoka, Fukushima-shi, Fukushima 960-1295, Japan"
date: 2026-06-18
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ../vancouver.csl
abstract: |
  **Introduction or background**: Ecological studies using aggregate prescription data often treat medication dispensing as a proxy for disease burden when diagnosis-linked records are unavailable. The degree to which outcome definition influences signal recovery in such designs has not been systematically quantified. We aimed to quantify how ecological inference changes when exposure variables, covariates, and regression frameworks are held constant while the medication-based outcome varies in pharmacological specificity.

  **Methods**: We conducted a cross-sectional ecological study across Japan's 47 prefectures (NDB Open Data, fiscal year 2023), using seasonal pollen exposure as a natural-experiment signal with an established biological mechanism. An allergy-specific outcome (antihistamines, ophthalmic, and nasal preparations; codes 441/449/131/132) and a pharmacologically heterogeneous comparator (topical anti-inflammatory agents; code 264) were evaluated under identical exposure variables, covariates, and regression frameworks. A pharmacologically unrelated negative control (oral hypoglycemic agents; code 396) assessed false-positive rates under the same analytic design.

  **Results**: The allergy-specific outcome achieved explained variance R^2^ = 0.469 versus R^2^ = 0.272 for the heterogeneous comparator (1.72-fold difference). Pollen associations were statistically significant in 7/8 sensitivity specifications for the allergy-specific outcome versus 5/6 for the best-performing comparator model. The negative control showed no pollen association in any specification, supporting that signal differences reflected outcome specificity rather than ecological confounding.

  **Discussion**: Outcome definition systematically shaped signal recovery before downstream modeling choices could be evaluated. Medication-based outcomes function as measurement instruments rather than interchangeable proxies for disease burden. Evaluating pharmacological specificity before model selection may improve the reliability of prescription-based ecological analyses when diagnosis linkage is unavailable. *Plain language summary*: When researchers count prescriptions instead of diagnoses, which drug category they choose can change study results a lot—even with the same pollen and statistical models. Narrow allergy drugs tracked seasonal pollen better than broad skin creams or diabetes medicines.
keywords:
  - outcome misclassification
  - ecological inference
  - prescription data
  - outcome definition
  - measurement error
  - allergic rhinitis
  - Japan
---

## What is new?

- Outcome definition changed recoverable ecological signal by 1.72-fold (R^2^ = 0.469 vs. 0.272) under identical exposure data, covariates, and regression frameworks.
- Pollen associations remained significant in 7/8 sensitivity specifications for the allergy-specific outcome but only 5/6 for the best comparator model, demonstrating specification-dependent attenuation with heterogeneous outcomes.
- A pharmacologically unrelated negative control (oral hypoglycaemic agents) showed no pollen association in any model, confirming that signal differences reflected outcome specificity rather than generic ecological confounding.
- Medication dispensing counts function as measurement instruments rather than interchangeable disease proxies in aggregate prescription data.
- Researchers using claims or open prescription aggregates should evaluate pharmacological specificity before model selection, interaction testing, or spatial refinement.

# Introduction

Administrative claims databases are increasingly used to study environmental determinants of population health [@benchimol2015record; @suto2024ndb]. In many settings, however, open or aggregate claims resources provide medication counts but not diagnosis-linked individual records. Researchers therefore often treat dispensing or prescribing volume as a proxy for disease burden. This strategy is attractive because it enables nationwide analyses at low cost, but it creates a central design problem: the validity of the outcome depends on how closely a pharmacological category maps to the disease process being studied.

Allergic rhinitis provides a useful natural experiment for examining this problem [@bousquet2008allergic; @okano2023guideline]. Seasonal pollen exposure is strongly linked to allergic symptoms, and Japan has a well-described burden of cedar and cypress pollinosis. Some medication categories, such as antihistamines and nasal or ophthalmic allergy preparations, have relatively narrow clinical indications and should therefore be sensitive to changes in allergic disease burden. Other categories, including broad topical anti-inflammatory preparations, are prescribed for heterogeneous dermatologic and inflammatory conditions and may contain only a small allergy-related component.

The present manuscript reframes a prefecture-level ecological pollen analysis as a methodological study of outcome misclassification [@copeland1977bias; @rothman2008modern; @lash2009bias]. Pollen is used as a case study because it provides a plausible environmental signal with strong seasonal and biological grounding; it is not the main contribution. The main question is how ecological inference changes when the exposure data, covariates, and regression framework are held constant while the medication-based outcome is changed from disease-aligned to pharmacologically heterogeneous. This design moves the claim beyond the unsurprising statement that specific outcomes are preferable: it asks how much inference is lost when the outcome is diluted.

We hypothesized that outcome definition would shape signal recovery before downstream modeling choices could be meaningfully interpreted. Specifically, a pollen association should be recoverable for allergy-specific prescriptions but attenuated for a broad topical anti-inflammatory comparator, even under identical environmental covariates, model hierarchy, and sensitivity procedures.

*Figure 1. Conceptual model: outcome definition as a measurement instrument*

# Methods

## Study Design and Unit of Analysis

We conducted a cross-sectional ecological study using Japan's 47 prefectures as the unit of analysis. All variables were aggregated at the prefecture level, corresponding to the administrative geography used in national statistics and NDB Open Data releases.

## Study Registration

The analysis plan for this inferential observational study was registered on the Open Science Framework (OSF; https://osf.io/yuc4a; registered 18 June 2026). The registration documents prespecified outcome definitions, hierarchical regression models, sensitivity analyses, and the negative-control design prior to manuscript submission to the *Journal of Clinical Epidemiology*.

## Prescription Outcomes

Prescription data were obtained from the NDB Open Data 10th edition, covering fiscal year 2023 [@mhlw2023ndb]. The high-specificity outcome was defined as the sum of three clinically allergy-oriented medication groups: antihistamines and anti-allergy agents (drug classification codes 441 and 449), ophthalmic preparations (code 131), and nasal preparations (code 132). These categories were standardized per 100,000 population.

The low-specificity comparator was defined as topical anti-inflammatory agents (code 264). This category was treated as a pharmacologically heterogeneous outcome because it includes medications used for multiple non-allergic indications. It therefore provides a negative-control-like contrast for assessing how outcome dilution affects ecological signal detection.

A second comparator was added to provide a pharmacologically unrelated negative control. Diabetes medications (drug classification code 396; oral hypoglycemic agents) were selected because seasonal pollen dispersal has no established biological pathway to diabetes management. Any pollen association detected for this outcome would therefore reflect confounding or chance rather than a disease-specific signal, enabling assessment of false-positive rates under the same analytic framework.

## Exposure Variables and Covariates

Pollen dispersal counts were obtained from Weathernews pollen observation data for the 2023 season and represented seasonal cumulative counts at prefecture-capital observation sites [@weathernews2023pollen]. PM~2.5~ and suspended particulate matter (SPM) concentrations were derived from Japan's atmospheric environmental observation system using multi-year prefecture averages from general monitoring stations [@nies2023air]. Aging rate and population density were included as demographic and urbanization covariates.

## Analytical Framework

The three analyses were independently conducted using the same NDB Open Data source, identical prefecture-level ecological units, the same pollen exposure data, parallel regression strategies, and overlapping covariate structures. The present study integrates these analyses to isolate the methodological effect of outcome definition rather than exposure specification. Because all three outcomes were derived from the same administrative data environment and evaluated against the same ecological exposure contrast, differences in signal recovery are interpreted primarily as evidence about measurement specificity rather than as evidence about different underlying pollen exposures.

The same hierarchical regression framework was applied to both outcome definitions. Baseline models estimated the association between pollen exposure and prescription outcome, followed by additive particulate-matter models, interaction models, and fully adjusted models including demographic covariates. PM~2.5~ and SPM were modeled in separate specifications because their high correlation makes simultaneous interpretation unstable.

## Robustness and Spatial Diagnostics

Robustness was assessed using heteroscedasticity-consistent standard errors, Cook's distance outlier exclusion, exclusion of Tokyo and Osaka as extreme population-density prefectures, removal of interaction terms, and log-transformation of prescription outcomes. Global Moran's I was used to assess spatial autocorrelation in outcomes and residuals. The key contrast was not whether every coefficient was statistically significant in a single specification, but whether the pollen signal could be recovered consistently across equivalent modeling choices when the outcome was clinically specific versus non-specific. Reporting followed STROBE and RECORD principles for observational studies using routinely collected health data [@benchimol2015record; @vonelm2007strobe].

# Results

## Outcome Specificity and Signal Recovery

Outcome definition changed ecological inference under a controlled analytic contrast. For the disease-aligned allergy-specific outcome, the full SPM model achieved R^2^ = 0.469 and retained a robust pollen signal across sensitivity analyses. For the pharmacologically heterogeneous code 264 comparator, the best-fitting model achieved R^2^ = 0.272, while the only specification with conventional pollen significance was the temperature-adjusted model (R^2^ = 0.237; pollen $\beta$ = 48.1 per 1,000 grains/cm^2^; p = 0.046). The best-model R^2^ gap was 1.72-fold, and the comparable-model R^2^ gap was 1.51-fold. This attenuation occurred despite identical exposure data and parallel analytical frameworks.

## Allergy-Specific Outcome

In the allergy-specific analysis, pollen exposure produced a recoverable and numerically interpretable signal. Pollen alone accounted for 11.0% of prefecture-level variation. In the fully adjusted PM~2.5~ specification, the model explained 35.7% of variation, with pollen significant under conventional standard errors but not under HC3 robust standard errors. In the SPM specification, model fit increased to 46.9%, pollen retained HC3 significance, and pollen remained significant across six sensitivity specifications (p = 0.006--0.045).

## Low-Specificity Comparator Outcome

The non-specific topical anti-inflammatory outcome recovered a weaker and more specification-dependent signal. Across the hierarchical model set (Supplementary Table S2), R^2^ ranged from 0.234 (Model 5, PM~2.5~) to 0.272 (Model 10, sunshine), with AIC values clustered between 1370.7 and 1373.8. Code 264 Model 10, the best-fitting sunshine model, achieved R^2^ = 0.272 but had a non-significant pollen association (p = 0.094). Code 264 Model 9, the temperature-adjusted model, was the only specification with conventional pollen significance (p = 0.046) and was significant in 5/6 sensitivity specifications (Supplementary Table S3), compared with 2/6 for the PM~2.5~ model and 3/6 for the SPM model.

## Sensitivity Analyses

Sensitivity analyses showed different stability profiles across outcome definitions. The allergy-specific outcome retained associations across robust standard errors, influential-observation checks, urban-prefecture exclusion, and transformed-outcome models. The heterogeneous comparator showed fewer stable pollen associations. Spatial autocorrelation diagnostics did not indicate residual dependence sufficient to explain the observed contrast.

## True Negative Control: Diabetes Medications

Diabetes medications (code 396) served as a pharmacologically unrelated negative control. Pollen exposure showed no significant association with diabetes prescription rates in any model specification. In the pollen-only model, pollen was non-significant (R^2^ = 0.029; p = 0.253). In the fully adjusted PM~2.5~ model (NC-5), pollen remained non-significant (R^2^ = 0.600; pollen p = 0.096), and in the SPM model (NC-6), pollen p = 0.075. The pollen × SPM interaction was clearly non-significant (p = 0.815). Sensitivity analyses confirmed the absence of a pollen signal: only 1 of 6 specifications yielded p < 0.05 for the pollen coefficient, compared with 6/6 for the allergy-specific outcome. High model R^2^ values for diabetes (0.58--0.60) were driven by aging rate (p < 0.001) and particulate matter concentrations (PM~2.5~ p = 0.002), not by pollen --- consistent with the known epidemiology of type 2 diabetes. This null result for a biologically unrelated outcome confirms that the pollen signal detected for allergy-specific prescriptions reflects outcome-specific pharmacoepidemiology rather than generic ecological confounding. Full results are presented in Supplementary Table S12.

# Discussion

## Principal Findings

This study shows that outcome definition can reshape ecological pharmacoepidemiological inference before any causal interpretation begins. Comparing three parallel analyses derived from the same national prescription source and pollen exposure framework, allergy-specific medication categories preserved a stronger and more robust signal than code 264 topical anti-inflammatory agents. The contribution is methodological: it demonstrates that a plausible environmental-health association can change in magnitude, precision, and robustness when the medication outcome moves from disease-aligned to pharmacologically heterogeneous.

## Why Outcome Specificity Matters

Medication-based outcomes are not interchangeable proxies for disease [@copeland1977bias; @rothman2008modern; @lash2009bias]. This may sound obvious, but its consequences are rarely quantified in ecological claims analyses. A narrow pharmacological category can function as a reasonably specific marker of clinical demand, whereas a broad category combines multiple indications, prescribing contexts, and care-seeking patterns. When only a subset of that category is environmentally responsive, the true association is averaged together with unrelated variation. The statistical consequence is lower explained variance, wider uncertainty, and reduced robustness across sensitivity checks; the interpretive consequence is that a plausible environmental signal may be mistaken for absence of association.

Although pollen exposure provides a biologically plausible case study, the methodological principle extends beyond allergic disease. The same measurement problem arises in ecological studies that use medication-based proxies for infectious diseases, chronic cardiometabolic conditions, mental health conditions, pain syndromes, and environmental exposures whenever diagnosis-linked records are unavailable [@benchimol2015record; @rothman2008modern; @lash2009bias]. In each setting, the medication category should be evaluated as a measurement instrument: a narrow outcome can preserve a disease-specific signal, whereas a heterogeneous category can dilute it before any causal model is fitted.

## Model Specification Is Secondary to Outcome Measurement

The particle-size comparison between PM~2.5~ and SPM is retained as a secondary case-study result rather than the central claim [@guarnieri2014outdoor; @phosri2017interactive; @konishi2014pollinosis]. In the disease-aligned allergy outcome, model specification still mattered: the SPM model fit better than the PM~2.5~ model and produced a more robust pollen coefficient. However, this result is interpretable only because the outcome first behaved as a plausible measurement instrument for allergic disease. The hierarchy is therefore methodological: outcome definition determines whether a signal is measurable; exposure-model specification then determines how sharply that signal is estimated.

## Implications for Claims-Based Environmental Health Research

For researchers using open claims databases, the first methodological decision should be the construction of the outcome. When diagnosis linkage is unavailable, pharmacological specificity should be evaluated before model selection, interaction testing, or spatial refinement. A useful workflow is to define a primary narrow outcome, analyze a broader comparator as a dilution or negative-control-like outcome, and interpret differences in signal recovery as evidence about outcome validity.

## Strengths and Limitations

Strengths of this study include nationwide coverage of all prefectures, a direct comparison of outcome definitions within the same data environment, and multiple sensitivity analyses. Limitations are inherent to the ecological design [@morgenstern1982ecologic; @robinson1950ecological; @wakefield2008ecologic; @elliott2004spatial]. The present study integrates two existing analyses rather than jointly re-estimating both outcomes within a single analytical pipeline; this design isolates outcome definition but may leave minor analytic differences unmeasured. Prefecture-level results cannot establish individual-level causal effects [@hernan2020causal], prescription counts do not capture over-the-counter medication use, and the comparator category may contain some allergy-related prescribing despite its broader indication range [@wakamiya2022tweets]. With N = 47 prefectures and multiple model specifications and sensitivity checks compared across both outcomes, p-values close to the conventional 0.05 threshold (e.g., the code 264 Model 9 pollen association, p = 0.046) should be interpreted descriptively and with appropriate caution rather than as definitive evidence of association. In addition, the drug-category subgroup analysis (Supplementary Table S11) indicates that within the allergy-specific outcome, the pollen association is driven primarily by antihistamines and anti-allergy agents (codes 441/449; p = 0.035), while the ophthalmic and nasal-spray components individually show no significant association; this heterogeneity within even the disease-aligned outcome suggests that outcome specificity operates on a continuum rather than as a binary distinction.

# Conclusions

These findings suggest that outcome definition may shape ecological pharmacoepidemiological inference even when exposure data, covariates, and model structure are held constant. In a nationwide prefecture-level analysis using pollen exposure as a case study, allergy-specific medication categories appeared to recover a quantifiable environmental-health signal, whereas a pharmacologically heterogeneous comparator appeared to attenuate it. When diagnosis-linked records are unavailable, testing whether medication categories behave as disease-aligned measurement instruments — rather than treating them as interchangeable proxies — may improve the reliability of claims-based ecological studies. Model specification remains important, but it may not rescue inference from a poorly aligned outcome. These results suggest that outcome specificity may warrant consideration as an early design decision in ecological inference rather than a downstream analytical choice.

@tbl-contrast summarizes the analytical contrast used to operationalize the outcome-specificity comparison.

# Acknowledgments

The authors thank the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare of Japan for providing access to the NDB Open Data. All data used are publicly available aggregate statistics.

# Conflicts of Interest

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

# Data Availability

The NDB Open Data used in this analysis are publicly available from the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare of Japan (https://www.mhlw.go.jp/stf/seisakunitsuite/bunya/0000177182.html). Analysis code is openly available on GitHub (https://github.com/haruki00430/NDB_pollen_allergy) and archived on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20747801).

# Author Contributions

Haruki Saito: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing -- original draft, Writing -- review and editing.

Tetsuya Ohira: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing -- review and editing.

# Funding

None declared.

# Declaration of Generative AI and AI-Assisted Technologies in the Manuscript Preparation Process

During the preparation and writing of this work, the authors used AI-assisted tools to support manuscript drafting and statistical analysis scripting. Cursor 3.0 (Anysphere) and Google Antigravity (Google) were used for AI-assisted writing and Python code development. Large language models used through these platforms included Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Claude Opus 4.8 (Anthropic) and GPT-5.5 (OpenAI) and Gemini 3 Pro (Google). These tools were used only for text drafting and code generation; no generative AI or AI-assisted tools were used to create, alter, or otherwise process any figures, images, or artwork in this manuscript. The authors reviewed and edited all AI-assisted outputs and were responsible for the study design, selection of statistical methods, interpretation of findings, conclusions, and final reference list. The authors take full responsibility for the integrity and accuracy of the final content. AI was not listed as an author.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

# Tables

::: {#tbl-contrast}

| Outcome definition | Clinical specificity | Expected signal behavior | Interpretation |
|:-------------------|:---------------------|:-------------------------|:---------------|
| Allergy-specific prescriptions: antihistamines/anti-allergy agents, ophthalmic preparations, nasal preparations | High | Pollen signal recoverable and more robust across model specifications | Primary disease-aligned outcome |
| Topical anti-inflammatory preparations | Low | Pollen signal attenuated under the same exposure and covariate framework | Heterogeneous comparator demonstrating outcome dilution |

: Table 1. Analytical contrast used to test outcome specificity {#tbl-contrast}

:::

# Supplementary Material

## Supplementary Table S1. Source-backed comparator outcome analysis (code 264)

These tables reproduce the key numerical results from the parallel code 264 topical anti-inflammatory analysis used as the heterogeneous comparator in the outcome-misclassification framework.

::: {#tbl-s1-comparison}

| Metric | Code 264 comparator | Allergy-specific outcome | Ratio / difference |
|:-------|:--------------------|:-------------------------|:-------------------|
| Best-fitting model | Model 10 (Sunshine) | Model 6 (SPM + interaction) | --- |
| R^2^ (best model) | 0.272 | 0.469 | 1.72× |
| R^2^ (comparable model) | 0.237 (Model 9 Temperature) | 0.357 (Model 5 PM~2.5~) | 1.51× |
| Sensitivity robustness | 5/6 specifications (83%) | 7/8 specifications (88%) | +5% |
| AIC (best model) | 1370.7 | 1365.0 (Model 6) | −5.7 |
| Moran residual p-values | 0.279--0.410 (NS) | Not assessed in retained source table | Descriptive only |
| R^2^ (comparable diabetes model) | NC-5: 0.600 (PM~2.5~); NC-6: 0.584 (SPM) | NC-6: 0.584 | High model fit driven by aging rate and particulate matter, not pollen |

: Supplementary Table S1. Outcome-specificity comparison metrics {#tbl-s1-comparison}

:::

## Supplementary Table S2. Hierarchical model performance for code 264 comparator outcome

::: {#tbl-s2-code264}

| Model | Core specification | R^2^ | AIC | Pollen p |
|:------|:-------------------|-----:|----:|---------:|
| Model 5 | Pollen + PM~2.5~ + interaction + covariates | 0.234 | 1373.8 | 0.060 |
| Model 6 | Pollen + SPM + interaction + covariates | 0.248 | 1372.6 | 0.055 |
| Model 7 | Pollen + humidity + interaction + covariates | 0.253 | 1371.9 | 0.131 |
| Model 9 | Pollen + temperature + interaction + covariates | 0.237 | 1372.9 | 0.046 |
| Model 10 | Pollen + sunshine + interaction + covariates | 0.272 | 1370.7 | 0.094 |
| Model 11 | Pollen + precipitation + interaction + covariates | 0.263 | 1371.3 | 0.051 |

: Supplementary Table S2. Hierarchical model performance for code 264 outcome {#tbl-s2-code264}

:::

## Supplementary Table S3. Sensitivity-analysis matrix for code 264 pollen coefficient p-values

::: {#tbl-s3-sensitivity264}

| Model | Baseline OLS | HC3 | Outlier excl. | Excl. Tokyo/Osaka | No interaction | Log transform | Sig. specs |
|:------|-------------:|----:|--------------:|------------------:|---------------:|--------------:|:----------:|
| M5 (PM~2.5~) | 0.060 | NS | NS | NS | NS | NS | 2/6 |
| M6 (SPM) | 0.055 | 0.044 | NS | 0.033 | 0.050 | NS | 3/6 |
| M7 (Humidity) | 0.131 | NS | NS | NS | NS | NS | 0/6 |
| M9 (Temperature) | 0.046 | 0.037 | 0.029 | 0.029 | 0.043 | 0.057 | 5/6 |
| M10 (Sunshine) | 0.094 | NS | NS | NS | NS | NS | 0/6 |
| M11 (Precipitation) | 0.051 | NS | NS | NS | NS | NS | --- |

: Supplementary Table S3. Sensitivity analysis for code 264 pollen p-values {#tbl-s3-sensitivity264}

:::

## Supplementary Source Tables (S4--S11): Allergy-Specific Outcome

Supplementary Tables S4--S11 retain the allergy-specific source analyses that underpin the integrated comparison.

### Supplementary Table S4. Descriptive statistics (N = 47 prefectures)

::: {#tbl-s4-descriptive}

| Variable | Mean | SD | Min | Max |
|:---------|-----:|---:|----:|----:|
| Total allergy Rx (per 100k) | 5,513,772 | 598,704 | 3,988,207 | 6,872,987 |
| Antihistamines (per 100k) | 5,210,378 | 599,237 | 3,672,817 | 6,646,422 |
| Ophthalmic (per 100k) | 272,074 | 117,500 | 106,747 | 593,823 |
| Nasal sprays (per 100k) | 31,319 | 14,238 | 10,144 | 87,291 |
| Pollen count (grains/cm^2^) | 7,079 | 3,235 | 0 | 17,827 |
| Forest ratio (%) | 62.5 | 15.4 | 28.8 | 83.6 |
| PM~2.5~ (μg/m^3^) | 8.34 | 1.20 | 6.04 | 10.93 |
| SPM (mg/m^3^) | 0.013 | 0.002 | 0.008 | 0.016 |
| Aging rate (%) | 31.1 | 3.2 | 22.9 | 38.1 |
| Population density (per km^2^) | 655 | 1,220 | 66 | 6,386 |

: Supplementary Table S4. Descriptive statistics {#tbl-s4-descriptive}

:::

### Supplementary Table S5. Hierarchical OLS regression results (allergy-specific outcome)

::: {#tbl-s5-hierarchy}

| Model | Specification | R^2^ | Adj. R^2^ | AIC | Pollen p (OLS) |
|:------|:-------------|-----:|----------:|----:|---------------:|
| 1 | Pollen only | 0.110 | 0.090 | 1381.4 | 0.023 |
| 2 | PM~2.5~ only | 0.022 | 0.001 | 1385.7 | --- |
| 3 | Pollen + PM~2.5~ | 0.132 | 0.092 | 1382.2 | 0.023 |
| 4 | Pollen + PM~2.5~ + interaction | 0.133 | 0.072 | 1384.1 | 0.037 |
| 5 | Full (PM~2.5~) | 0.357 | 0.279 | 1374.0 | 0.014 |
| **6** | **Full (SPM)** | **0.469** | **0.404** | **1365.0** | **0.008** |

: Supplementary Table S5. Model hierarchy (allergy-specific outcome) {#tbl-s5-hierarchy}

:::

### Supplementary Table S6. Coefficients for the best-fitting model (Model 6: SPM specification)

::: {#tbl-s6-model6coef}

| Variable | $\beta$ | SE | 95% CI | p |
|:---------|--------:|---:|:-------|--:|
| Intercept | 7,248,585 | 1,233,257 | [4,757,971; 9,739,199] | < 0.001 |
| Pollen count | 59.5 | 21.4 | [16.2; 102.7] | 0.008 |
| SPM | −68,668,596 | 39,587,978 | [−148,618,140; 11,280,948] | 0.090 |
| Pollen × SPM | 33,247 | 13,697 | [5,586; 60,908] | 0.020 |
| Aging rate | −45,011 | 31,666 | [−108,962; 18,941] | 0.163 |
| Population density | 181.9 | 75.3 | [29.7; 334.0] | 0.020 |

: Supplementary Table S6. Model 6 coefficients {#tbl-s6-model6coef}

:::

### Supplementary Table S7. p-values: OLS vs HC3 robust standard errors

::: {#tbl-s7-hc3}

| Variable | M5 OLS | M5 HC3 | M6 OLS | M6 HC3 |
|:---------|-------:|-------:|-------:|-------:|
| Pollen count | 0.014 | 0.082 | 0.008 | **0.033** |
| Interaction | 0.985 | 0.991 | 0.020 | 0.066 |
| Population density | 0.004 | 0.029 | 0.020 | **0.006** |

: Supplementary Table S7. OLS vs HC3 p-value comparison {#tbl-s7-hc3}

:::

### Supplementary Table S8. Sensitivity analysis for Model 6 (SPM specification)

::: {#tbl-s8-sensm6}

| Specification | N | R^2^ | Pollen p | Interaction p | Note |
|:--------------|--:|-----:|---------:|:-------------:|:-----|
| Baseline (OLS) | 47 | 0.469 | 0.008 | 0.020 | |
| HC3 robust SE | 47 | 0.469 | 0.033 | 0.066 | Mild heteroscedasticity |
| Excl. Cook's D outliers | 45 | 0.431 | 0.045 | 0.142 | Yamanashi, Okinawa excluded |
| Excl. Tokyo & Osaka | 45 | 0.389 | 0.010 | 0.028 | |
| No interaction term | 47 | 0.393 | 0.015 | --- | SPM main effect p = 0.023 |
| Log-transformed outcome | 47 | 0.450 | 0.006 | 0.016 | |

: Supplementary Table S8. Sensitivity analysis, Model 6 {#tbl-s8-sensm6}

:::

### Supplementary Table S9. Sensitivity analysis for Model 5 (PM~2.5~ specification)

::: {#tbl-s9-sensm5}

| Specification | N | R^2^ | Pollen p | Interaction p |
|:--------------|--:|-----:|---------:|:-------------:|
| Baseline (OLS) | 47 | 0.357 | 0.014 | 0.985 |
| HC3 robust SE | 47 | 0.357 | 0.082 | 0.991 |
| Excl. Cook's D outliers | 43 | 0.486 | 0.014 | 0.333 |
| Excl. Tokyo & Osaka | 45 | 0.264 | 0.020 | 0.961 |
| No interaction term | 47 | 0.357 | 0.009 | --- |
| Log-transformed outcome | 47 | 0.331 | 0.013 | 0.907 |

: Supplementary Table S9. Sensitivity analysis, Model 5 {#tbl-s9-sensm5}

:::

### Supplementary Table S10. Comparison of pollen exposure indicators

::: {#tbl-s10-pollencomp}

| Indicator | R^2^ | Adj. R^2^ | AIC | ΔAIC |
|:----------|-----:|----------:|----:|-----:|
| Pollen count (Weather News) | 0.357 | 0.296 | 1372.0 | ref. |
| Forest area ratio (Forestry Agency) | 0.242 | 0.170 | 1379.8 | +7.7 |

: Supplementary Table S10. Pollen indicator comparison {#tbl-s10-pollencomp}

:::

### Supplementary Table S11. Drug category-specific OLS regression results

::: {#tbl-s11-subgroup}

| Drug category | R^2^ | Pollen $\beta$ | Pollen p |
|:--------------|-----:|---------------:|---------:|
| Antihistamines (441 + 449) | 0.129 | 60.3 | 0.035 |
| Ophthalmic (131) | 0.011 | −0.8 | 0.890 |
| Nasal sprays (132) | 0.020 | 0.0 | 0.971 |

: Supplementary Table S11. Subgroup analysis by drug category {#tbl-s11-subgroup}

:::

## Supplementary Table S12. Negative control analysis: diabetes medications (code 396)

Regression results for diabetes medications (code 396) as a pharmacologically unrelated negative control. Pollen exposure had no significant association in any fully adjusted model (NC-5 pollen p = 0.096; NC-6 pollen p = 0.075). Only 1 of 6 sensitivity specifications reached p < 0.05, confirming that the allergy-specific pollen signal does not reflect generic ecological confounding. High R^2^ values for the diabetes outcome (0.58--0.60) were attributable to aging rate (p < 0.001) and particulate matter, not pollen.

::: {#tbl-s12-diabetes}

| Model | Specification | R^2^ | Pollen p | Note |
|:------|:-------------|-----:|---------:|:-----|
| NC-1 | Pollen only | 0.029 | 0.253 | Non-significant |
| NC-5 | Full PM~2.5~ model | 0.600 | 0.096 | Aging rate p < 0.001 |
| NC-6 | Full SPM model | 0.584 | 0.075 | Pollen × SPM p = 0.815 |
| Sensitivity (NC-5 base) | 6 specifications | --- | --- | 1/6 significant (p < 0.05) |

: Supplementary Table S12. Diabetes medications negative control {#tbl-s12-diabetes}

:::
